era_context: Late 15th century: Portuguese maritime expansion accelerating; Ottoman consolidation in the eastern Mediterranean; Italian Renaissance flourishing; Ming China under the Hongwu and Yongle successors; Aztec Empire under Ahuitzotl; the Iberian Reconquista in its final phase; England under Henry VII consolidating Tudor rule.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written documentation; indigenous records sparse)
Interior Southeast Asia (limited European contact; local chronicles underrepresented)

events.title: Ahuitzotl accedes to the Aztec throne
events.description: Ahuitzotl (r. 1486–1502) ascended to the Aztec throne following the death of his predecessor Tizoc. Ahuitzotl was known for his military prowess and aggressive expansionism, and his reign marked a period of significant territorial expansion and cultural flourishing for the Aztec Empire. He undertook major construction projects in Tenochtitlan, including the expansion and dedication of the Templo Mayor. His reign represented the apex of Aztec power before the arrival of Spanish conquistadors.

sources.name: Codex Mendoza (compiled post-conquest but based on pre-Columbian sources)
Sahagún, Florentine Codex (16th-century compilation of Nahua accounts)

events.certainty_note: Ahuitzotl's accession is attested in post-conquest sources; the specific dating to 1486 is based on historiographic consensus, though some sources place it in 1485 or 1487.

events.title: Diet of Worms convened by Maximilian I
events.description: Maximilian I convened the Diet of Worms in 1486 to address issues of imperial governance, taxation, and military organization. The diet was part of Maximilian's efforts to centralize imperial authority and reform the structure of the Holy Roman Empire. The diet resulted in various decrees and agreements that strengthened imperial power, though the fragmented nature of the empire limited the effectiveness of these reforms.

sources.name: Imperial diet records and contemporary chronicles
Historiographic consensus (multiple modern histories of the Holy Roman Empire)

events.certainty_note: The Diet of Worms is well-documented in imperial records.
cross_references: 1488-002 (Maximilian I's election as Holy Roman Emperor)

events.title: Christopher Columbus seeks patronage for his westward voyage
events.description: Christopher Columbus, an Italian-born navigator, began seeking patronage for his proposed westward voyage to reach Asia. Columbus had previously approached the Portuguese court under King John II, but was rejected. In 1486, Columbus presented his proposal to the Spanish monarchs Isabella and Ferdinand. The Spanish court was initially skeptical, but Columbus's persistence and the eventual support of influential courtiers led to further consideration of his proposal. The Spanish monarchs would eventually approve Columbus's voyage in 1492.

sources.name: Columbus's own writings and later biographical accounts
Spanish royal records and contemporary chronicles

events.certainty_note: Columbus's early efforts to seek patronage are attested in various sources; the specific dating of 1486 is based on historiographic consensus, though some sources place his initial approach to the Spanish court slightly earlier or later.

events.title: Portuguese exploration of the African coast continues under John II
events.description: King John II of Portugal continued his systematic patronage of maritime exploration along the African coast. Portuguese explorers, including Diogo Cão and others, were mapping the African coastline and establishing trading posts and diplomatic contacts with local rulers. These expeditions were part of Portugal's broader strategy to find a sea route to India and to establish a monopoly on African trade. By 1486, Portuguese exploration had reached the southern regions of West-Central Africa.

sources.name: Portuguese royal records and navigational accounts
Later Portuguese chronicles and historiographic accounts

events.certainty_note: The broader Portuguese exploration program is well-documented; specific events in 1486 are less precisely dated.

events.title: Ottoman-Mamluk War begins
events.description: The Ottoman-Mamluk War (1485–1491) began as tensions between the Ottoman Empire and the Mamluk Sultanate of Egypt escalated into open conflict. The war was fought over control of trade routes, territorial influence, and regional hegemony in the eastern Mediterranean and the Levant. The conflict resulted in Ottoman victory and the incorporation of the Levant and Egypt into the Ottoman Empire, marking a significant shift in the balance of power in the eastern Mediterranean and the Middle East.

sources.name: Ottoman and Mamluk chronicles and diplomatic records
Historiographic consensus (multiple modern histories of the Ottoman and Mamluk empires)

events.certainty_note: The Ottoman-Mamluk War is well-documented; the specific dating of its beginning to 1485 or 1486 varies slightly among sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Ahuitzotl's accession is uncertain; some sources place it in 1485 or 1487 rather than 1486. Columbus's early efforts to seek patronage are sometimes romanticized as a clear narrative of rejection and eventual triumph; modern scholarship emphasizes the complexity of his negotiations and the role of various courtiers and advisors. The beginning of the Ottoman-Mamluk War is sometimes dated to 1485 rather than 1486, reflecting uncertainty in the historical record.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1486 is rich for Europe (particularly Spain, Portugal, and the Holy Roman Empire) due to extensive diplomatic archives, royal records, and contemporary chronicles. The Ottoman and Mamluk sources are also well-preserved. The surviving record is heavily weighted toward elite political and military events; economic, social, and cultural history is less well-attested. Sub-Saharan Africa, interior Southeast Asia, and the Pacific remain poorly documented for this year. Mesoamerican history relies heavily on post-conquest compilations and codices, which introduce chronological uncertainty.

graph_edges.note: Ahuitzotl's accession began his reign of expansion and cultural flourishing
Columbus's efforts to seek patronage in 1486 eventually led to the approval of his voyage in 1492
The Ottoman-Mamluk War (1485–1491) resulted in Ottoman victory and expansion
